instruction: Implement the PIMPL (Pointer to Implementation) idiom in C++ to achieve ABI stability and reduced compilation dependencies.
output:
<think>
PIMPL (Pointer to Implementation) is a C++ technique that hides implementation details behind an opaque pointer. This achieves two goals: ABI stability (changing implementation doesn't break binary compatibility) and compilation firewall (changes to implementation don't trigger recompilation of clients).

The idea: the public header declares only a forward-declared pointer to an implementation class. All private members live in the implementation class, defined only in the .cpp file. The public class delegates to the impl.

Key design decisions:
1. Use std::unique_ptr for the impl pointer (RAII, move semantics)
2. Must define destructor in .cpp (where Impl is complete type)
3. Must define copy/move operations explicitly if needed
4. The impl class holds all private data members and helper methods
5. For ABI stability: only add virtual methods at the end, never remove

I'll show a complete PIMPL implementation with proper copy/move semantics.
</think>

```cpp
// ============================================================
// widget.h — Public header (stable ABI, minimal includes)
// ============================================================
#pragma once
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

// Forward declare — no need to include implementation headers
class WidgetImpl;

class Widget {
public:
    // Constructor/destructor must be declared here, defined in .cpp
    explicit Widget(std::string name, int width = 100, int height = 100);
    ~Widget();  // Must be in .cpp where WidgetImpl is complete

    // Move operations (efficient)
    Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept;

    // Copy operations (deep copy the impl)
    Widget(const Widget& other);
    Widget& operator=(const Widget& other);

    // Public API — stable, changes here break ABI
    void setName(const std::string& name);
    [[nodiscard]] std::string getName() const;

    void resize(int width, int height);
    [[nodiscard]] int getWidth() const;
    [[nodiscard]] int getHeight() const;

    void addChild(const std::string& childName);
    [[nodiscard]] std::vector<std::string> getChildren() const;

    void render() const;

private:
    // The only private member — pointer to implementation
    std::unique_ptr<WidgetImpl> pImpl;
};

// ============================================================
// widget.cpp — Implementation (can change without breaking ABI)
// ============================================================
#include "widget.h"
#include <iostream>
#include <algorithm>
// Can include any headers here without affecting clients
// #include <some_heavy_library.h>

// The actual implementation class — completely hidden
class WidgetImpl {
public:
    std::string name;
    int width;
    int height;
    std::vector<std::string> children;
    int renderCount = 0;  // Can add fields without ABI break!

    WidgetImpl(std::string n, int w, int h)
        : name(std::move(n)), width(w), height(h) {}

    // Helper methods — private to implementation
    void validateDimensions() {
        width = std::max(1, width);
        height = std::max(1, height);
    }

    void renderInternal() const {
        std::cout << "Rendering '" << name << "' ("
                  << width << "x" << height << ") with "
                  << children.size() << " children\n";
    }
};

// Constructor: creates the impl
Widget::Widget(std::string name, int width, int height)
    : pImpl(std::make_unique<WidgetImpl>(std::move(name), width, height)) {
    pImpl->validateDimensions();
}

// Destructor: must be here where WidgetImpl is a complete type
Widget::~Widget() = default;

// Move operations
Widget::Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept = default;

// Copy: deep copy the impl
Widget::Widget(const Widget& other)
    : pImpl(std::make_unique<WidgetImpl>(*other.pImpl)) {}

Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other) {
    if (this != &other) {
        pImpl = std::make_unique<WidgetImpl>(*other.pImpl);
    }
    return *this;
}

// Public methods delegate to impl
void Widget::setName(const std::string& name) { pImpl->name = name; }
std::string Widget::getName() const { return pImpl->name; }

void Widget::resize(int width, int height) {
    pImpl->width = width;
    pImpl->height = height;
    pImpl->validateDimensions();
}

int Widget::getWidth() const { return pImpl->width; }
int Widget::getHeight() const { return pImpl->height; }

void Widget::addChild(const std::string& childName) {
    pImpl->children.push_back(childName);
}

std::vector<std::string> Widget::getChildren() const {
    return pImpl->children;
}

void Widget::render() const {
    pImpl->renderCount++;  // Mutable through const — impl detail
    pImpl->renderInternal();
}

// ============================================================
// Benefits demonstrated:
// 1. Add renderCount field — no ABI break, no recompilation
// 2. Add validateDimensions() — internal method, invisible
// 3. Include heavy headers — only widget.cpp recompiles
// 4. sizeof(Widget) is always sizeof(unique_ptr) — stable
// ============================================================
```

PIMPL provides ABI stability because `sizeof(Widget)` never changes (it's always the size of one `unique_ptr`). Adding fields to `WidgetImpl`, changing internal algorithms, or including new headers in the .cpp file never triggers recompilation of files that include `widget.h`. The cost is one heap allocation per object and one pointer indirection per method call — usually negligible. The Rule of Five is handled: move operations are defaulted, copy does deep copy of the impl, destructor is defaulted in the .cpp where `WidgetImpl` is complete.

instruction: Design a C++ header organization strategy for large projects that minimizes compilation time and manages dependencies cleanly.
output:
<think>
Header organization in C++ directly impacts compilation time. Bad practices (including everything everywhere) can make builds 10x slower. Let me reason through the best practices:

1. Forward declarations — declare types without including their full definition when only pointers/references are needed.
2. Include what you use (IWYU) — every file includes exactly what it needs, nothing more.
3. Header guards — #pragma once or include guards.
4. Separate public/private headers — public API in include/, implementation details in src/.
5. Precompiled headers (PCH) — for stable, frequently-included headers (STL, framework).
6. Module interface (C++20) — the modern replacement for headers.

The goal is to minimize the transitive include closure: when you change one header, how many files need to recompile?

Strategies:
- Use forward declarations in headers, full includes in .cpp files
- Keep headers small and focused (one class per header)
- Use PIMPL for implementation hiding
- Separate interface headers from implementation headers
- Use a stable public API header set that changes rarely
</think>

```cpp
// ============================================================
// Project structure:
//   include/mylib/       — Public headers (installed/exported)
//     mylib.h            — Umbrella header
//     fwd.h              — Forward declarations
//     widget.h           — Widget public API
//     renderer.h         — Renderer public API
//   src/                  — Private implementation
//     widget_impl.h      — Widget internals (NOT installed)
//     widget.cpp
//     renderer.cpp
//     detail/             — Internal utilities
//       memory_pool.h
// ============================================================

// --- include/mylib/fwd.h --- FORWARD DECLARATIONS
// Include this instead of full headers when you only need types
#pragma once

namespace mylib {

class Widget;
class Renderer;
class Canvas;
class Theme;

// Forward declare enums with underlying type (C++11)
enum class RenderMode : int;
enum class WidgetState : int;

// Forward declare templates
template<typename T> class ResourceHandle;

}  // namespace mylib

// --- include/mylib/widget.h --- PUBLIC HEADER
#pragma once

#include <string>
#include <memory>
#include <functional>
#include "mylib/fwd.h"  // Forward declarations, not full includes

namespace mylib {

enum class WidgetState : int {
    Idle,
    Hovered,
    Active,
    Disabled,
};

// Public API — stable interface
class Widget {
public:
    explicit Widget(std::string id);
    ~Widget();

    Widget(Widget&&) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept;

    // Uses forward-declared types — no need to include renderer.h
    void attachRenderer(Renderer* renderer);
    void setTheme(std::shared_ptr<Theme> theme);

    void setOnClick(std::function<void(Widget&)> callback);
    [[nodiscard]] const std::string& id() const;
    [[nodiscard]] WidgetState state() const;

    void render(Canvas& canvas) const;  // Canvas passed by ref — fwd decl sufficient

private:
    struct Impl;  // PIMPL — forward declared here
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> pImpl_;
};

}  // namespace mylib

// --- src/widget_impl.h --- PRIVATE HEADER (not installed)
#pragma once

#include "mylib/widget.h"
#include "mylib/renderer.h"  // Full include — only in private header
#include "detail/memory_pool.h"
#include <vector>
#include <unordered_map>

namespace mylib {

// Implementation details — hidden from public API
struct Widget::Impl {
    std::string id;
    WidgetState state = WidgetState::Idle;
    Renderer* renderer = nullptr;
    std::shared_ptr<Theme> theme;
    std::function<void(Widget&)> onClick;
    std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Widget>> children;
    detail::MemoryPool allocator;  // Internal utility

    void updateLayout();
    void handleEvent(int eventType);
};

}  // namespace mylib

// --- src/widget.cpp ---
#include "widget_impl.h"  // Private header with full includes
#include "mylib/renderer.h"
#include "mylib/canvas.h"

namespace mylib {

Widget::Widget(std::string id)
    : pImpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>()) {
    pImpl_->id = std::move(id);
}

Widget::~Widget() = default;
Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default;

void Widget::attachRenderer(Renderer* renderer) {
    pImpl_->renderer = renderer;
}

void Widget::setTheme(std::shared_ptr<Theme> theme) {
    pImpl_->theme = std::move(theme);
}

void Widget::setOnClick(std::function<void(Widget&)> callback) {
    pImpl_->onClick = std::move(callback);
}

const std::string& Widget::id() const { return pImpl_->id; }
WidgetState Widget::state() const { return pImpl_->state; }

void Widget::render(Canvas& canvas) const {
    if (pImpl_->renderer) {
        // Full Renderer API available here (included in widget_impl.h)
        // pImpl_->renderer->draw(canvas, *this);
    }
}

// --- CMakeLists.txt snippet for precompiled headers ---
// target_precompile_headers(mylib PRIVATE
//     <string>
//     <vector>
//     <memory>
//     <functional>
//     <unordered_map>
// )

}  // namespace mylib
```

Header organization strategy: (1) `fwd.h` provides forward declarations — include this when you only need type names (pointers, references). (2) Public headers in `include/` use minimal includes + forward declarations. (3) Private headers in `src/` include full definitions. (4) PIMPL hides implementation from the public header. (5) Precompiled headers for STL types eliminate redundant parsing. Changing `widget_impl.h` or `detail/memory_pool.h` only recompiles `widget.cpp` — not any file that includes `widget.h`.

instruction: Implement a dependency management strategy in C++ using modern CMake with interface libraries, exported targets, and version-compatible package configs.
output:
<think>
Modern CMake (3.x+) has transformed C++ dependency management. The key concepts:

1. Target-based dependency — `target_link_libraries` propagates include paths, compile flags, and link libraries.
2. Interface libraries — header-only libraries as targets.
3. Exported targets — let consumers `find_package` your library and get all compile/link info.
4. Version compatibility — semantic versioning in package configs.
5. FetchContent — download dependencies at configure time.

The goal is: a consumer should be able to `find_package(MyLib)` and `target_link_libraries(app MyLib::MyLib)` and everything just works — include paths, link flags, compile definitions.

I'll show a complete CMake setup for a library with proper export, versioning, and consumption patterns.
</think>

```cmake
# ============================================================
# Library CMakeLists.txt — Proper modern CMake
# ============================================================
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)
project(MyLib VERSION 2.1.0 LANGUAGES CXX)

# Set C++ standard as an interface requirement
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)

# Define the library target
add_library(mylib
    src/widget.cpp
    src/renderer.cpp
    src/canvas.cpp
)
add_library(MyLib::MyLib ALIAS mylib)  # Namespace alias

# Public headers: propagated to consumers via target_link_libraries
target_include_directories(mylib
    PUBLIC
        $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include>
        $<INSTALL_INTERFACE:include>
    PRIVATE
        ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/src  # Private headers
)

# Compile features propagated to consumers
target_compile_features(mylib PUBLIC cxx_std_20)

# Platform-specific compile definitions
target_compile_definitions(mylib
    PUBLIC
        $<$<PLATFORM_ID:Windows>:MYLIB_PLATFORM_WINDOWS>
        $<$<PLATFORM_ID:Linux>:MYLIB_PLATFORM_LINUX>
    PRIVATE
        MYLIB_BUILDING_LIB  # Only for building, not consuming
)

# ============================================================
# Header-only utility library (interface library)
# ============================================================
add_library(mylib_utils INTERFACE)
add_library(MyLib::Utils ALIAS mylib_utils)

target_include_directories(mylib_utils
    INTERFACE
        $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include>
        $<INSTALL_INTERFACE:include>
)

target_compile_features(mylib_utils INTERFACE cxx_std_17)

# ============================================================
# Dependencies — FetchContent for third-party
# ============================================================
include(FetchContent)

FetchContent_Declare(fmt
    GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/fmtlib/fmt.git
    GIT_TAG        10.1.1
    GIT_SHALLOW    TRUE
)
FetchContent_MakeAvailable(fmt)

# Link dependency — PUBLIC propagates to consumers
target_link_libraries(mylib
    PUBLIC
        MyLib::Utils     # Our header-only lib
    PRIVATE
        fmt::fmt         # fmt is implementation detail
)

# ============================================================
# Install & Export — Let consumers find_package(MyLib)
# ============================================================
include(GNUInstallDirs)
include(CMakePackageConfigHelpers)

# Install library and headers
install(TARGETS mylib mylib_utils
    EXPORT MyLibTargets
    LIBRARY DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
    ARCHIVE DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
    RUNTIME DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_BINDIR}
    INCLUDES DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR}
)

install(DIRECTORY include/
    DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR}
)

# Export targets file
install(EXPORT MyLibTargets
    FILE MyLibTargets.cmake
    NAMESPACE MyLib::
    DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
)

# Version compatibility file
write_basic_package_version_file(
    "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfigVersion.cmake"
    VERSION ${PROJECT_VERSION}
    COMPATIBILITY SameMajorVersion  # 2.x compatible with 2.y
)

# Config file
configure_package_config_file(
    "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/cmake/MyLibConfig.cmake.in"
    "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfig.cmake"
    INSTALL_DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
)

install(FILES
    "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfig.cmake"
    "${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfigVersion.cmake"
    DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
)
```

```cpp
// --- cmake/MyLibConfig.cmake.in ---
// @PACKAGE_INIT@
// include("${CMAKE_CURRENT_LIST_DIR}/MyLibTargets.cmake")
// check_required_components(MyLib)

// --- Consumer CMakeLists.txt ---
// find_package(MyLib 2.0 REQUIRED)  # Finds 2.x, rejects 3.x
// target_link_libraries(my_app PRIVATE MyLib::MyLib)
// # Automatically gets: include paths, compile features, link libs
```

Modern CMake dependency management: targets carry their own requirements (include paths via `target_include_directories`, features via `target_compile_features`, definitions via `target_compile_definitions`). PUBLIC propagates to consumers, PRIVATE stays internal. Generator expressions (`$<BUILD_INTERFACE:...>`) handle the difference between build-tree and install-tree paths. The export system creates importable targets with `MyLib::` namespace, and `SameMajorVersion` compatibility means 2.1.0 is compatible with any 2.x consumer.

instruction: Design an ABI-stable C++ plugin system where plugins compiled with different compiler versions can safely interact through a C-compatible interface with version negotiation.
output:
<think>
ABI stability is a critical concern for C++ plugin systems. Different compilers (or even different versions of the same compiler) may have incompatible C++ ABIs — different vtable layouts, name mangling, exception handling, memory layouts for STL types.

The solution: use a C-compatible interface at the plugin boundary. C ABI is stable across compilers. Then build a C++ wrapper on top for ergonomics.

Key design decisions:
1. Plugin interface is pure C (extern "C") — no C++ types cross the boundary
2. Version negotiation — host and plugin agree on API version
3. No STL types cross the boundary — use C arrays, char*, etc.
4. Memory ownership is explicit — allocator/deallocator pairs
5. Error handling via return codes, not exceptions
6. Plugin provides a factory function that returns a vtable-like struct

The vtable approach: define a struct of function pointers. This is the C equivalent of a virtual interface. The plugin fills in the function pointers, and the host calls through them.
</think>

```cpp
// ============================================================
// plugin_api.h — C-compatible plugin interface (ABI stable)
// This header is shared between host and plugins
// ============================================================
#pragma once

#include <stdint.h>
#include <stddef.h>

#ifdef __cplusplus
extern "C" {
#endif

// API version for compatibility checking
#define PLUGIN_API_VERSION_MAJOR 1
#define PLUGIN_API_VERSION_MINOR 0

typedef enum {
    PLUGIN_OK = 0,
    PLUGIN_ERROR = -1,
    PLUGIN_VERSION_MISMATCH = -2,
    PLUGIN_NOT_FOUND = -3,
} PluginResult;

typedef struct {
    uint32_t major;
    uint32_t minor;
} PluginVersion;

// Plugin info — returned by the plugin
typedef struct {
    const char* name;          // Static string, plugin-owned
    const char* description;
    PluginVersion version;
    PluginVersion api_version; // Must match host
} PluginInfo;

// Plugin vtable — function pointers for plugin operations
// This is the ABI-stable "virtual interface"
typedef struct PluginInterface {
    // Initialize plugin with host-provided config (JSON string)
    PluginResult (*init)(void* ctx, const char* config_json);

    // Process data — input and output are opaque byte buffers
    PluginResult (*process)(
        void* ctx,
        const uint8_t* input, size_t input_len,
        uint8_t** output, size_t* output_len  // Plugin allocates
    );

    // Free memory allocated by the plugin
    void (*free_buffer)(void* ctx, uint8_t* buffer);

    // Get plugin status as JSON string (plugin-owned, valid until next call)
    const char* (*get_status)(void* ctx);

    // Shutdown and cleanup
    void (*shutdown)(void* ctx);
} PluginInterface;

// The single entry point every plugin must export
// Returns plugin info and fills in the interface vtable
typedef PluginResult (*PluginEntryFunc)(
    PluginInfo* info_out,
    PluginInterface* iface_out,
    void** ctx_out  // Opaque context pointer
);

// Well-known symbol name for the entry point
#define PLUGIN_ENTRY_SYMBOL "plugin_entry"

#ifdef __cplusplus
}
#endif

// ============================================================
// plugin_host.hpp — C++ host wrapper (ergonomic API)
// ============================================================
#pragma once

#include "plugin_api.h"
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <memory>
#include <stdexcept>

#ifdef _WIN32
#include <windows.h>
#else
#include <dlfcn.h>
#endif

class PluginHost {
public:
    struct LoadedPlugin {
        std::string path;
        PluginInfo info;
        PluginInterface iface;
        void* ctx;
        void* lib_handle;
    };

    LoadedPlugin load(const std::string& path) {
        // Load shared library
        void* handle = loadLibrary(path);
        if (!handle) {
            throw std::runtime_error("Failed to load: " + path);
        }

        // Find entry point
        auto entry = (PluginEntryFunc)getSymbol(handle, PLUGIN_ENTRY_SYMBOL);
        if (!entry) {
            closeLibrary(handle);
            throw std::runtime_error("Missing entry point: " + path);
        }

        // Call entry point
        LoadedPlugin plugin{};
        plugin.path = path;
        plugin.lib_handle = handle;

        PluginResult res = entry(&plugin.info, &plugin.iface, &plugin.ctx);
        if (res != PLUGIN_OK) {
            closeLibrary(handle);
            throw std::runtime_error("Plugin init failed: " + std::to_string(res));
        }

        // Version check
        if (plugin.info.api_version.major != PLUGIN_API_VERSION_MAJOR) {
            closeLibrary(handle);
            throw std::runtime_error("API version mismatch");
        }

        return plugin;
    }

    std::vector<uint8_t> process(LoadedPlugin& p, const std::vector<uint8_t>& input) {
        uint8_t* output = nullptr;
        size_t output_len = 0;

        PluginResult res = p.iface.process(
            p.ctx, input.data(), input.size(), &output, &output_len
        );
        if (res != PLUGIN_OK) {
            throw std::runtime_error("Plugin process failed");
        }

        std::vector<uint8_t> result(output, output + output_len);
        p.iface.free_buffer(p.ctx, output);  // Plugin frees its own memory
        return result;
    }

    void unload(LoadedPlugin& p) {
        if (p.iface.shutdown) p.iface.shutdown(p.ctx);
        closeLibrary(p.lib_handle);
    }

private:
    static void* loadLibrary(const std::string& path) {
        #ifdef _WIN32
        return LoadLibraryA(path.c_str());
        #else
        return dlopen(path.c_str(), RTLD_NOW);
        #endif
    }

    static void* getSymbol(void* handle, const char* name) {
        #ifdef _WIN32
        return (void*)GetProcAddress((HMODULE)handle, name);
        #else
        return dlsym(handle, name);
        #endif
    }

    static void closeLibrary(void* handle) {
        #ifdef _WIN32
        FreeLibrary((HMODULE)handle);
        #else
        dlclose(handle);
        #endif
    }
};
```

The plugin system is ABI-stable because only C types cross the shared library boundary: function pointers, plain structs, `const char*`, and `uint8_t*` buffers. No C++ types (string, vector, exceptions) cross the boundary — each side wraps in its own C++ types. Memory ownership is explicit: the plugin allocates output buffers, provides a `free_buffer` function for the host to release them. Version negotiation via `api_version` ensures incompatible plugins are rejected at load time.
